questions.text: Which one of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The competitive inhibitor does not affect the rate of breakdown of the enzyme-substrate complex
options.explanation: Correct. Competitive inhibitors primarily compete with the substrate for the active site but do not directly affect the breakdown of the enzyme-substrate complex once it has formed.

options.text: The presence of the competitive inhibitor decreases the Km of the enzyme for the substrate
options.explanation: Incorrect. The presence of a competitive inhibitor increases the Km because a higher concentration of substrate is needed to achieve half-maximal velocity (Vmax). This statement inaccurately describes the effect of competitive inhibitors on Km.

options.text: A competitive inhibitor reacts reversibly with the enzyme to form an enzyme-inhibitor complex
options.explanation: Correct. Competitive inhibitors bind reversibly to the active site, forming a complex that prevents substrate binding.

options.text: In competitive inhibition, the inhibitor molecule is not chemically changed by the enzyme
options.explanation: Correct. Competitive inhibitors are not chemically altered by the enzyme; they simply occupy the active site, blocking substrate binding. Once the inhibitor is removed, the enzyme can function normally.

questions.explanation: Competitive inhibition is a reversible inhibition where the inhibitor competes with the normal substrate for the active site of the enzyme. A competitive inhibitor is usually chemically similar to the normal substrate and therefore, fits into the active site of an enzyme and binds with it. The inhibition is thus due to substrate analog. The enzyme, now cannot act upon the substrate, and reaction products are not formed. Incorrect. In competitive inhibition, the Km (Michaelis constant) increases because a higher concentration of substrate is required to reach half-maximal velocity (Vmax) in the presence of the inhibitor. This statement misrepresents the effect of competitive inhibitors on Km. Km value or Michaelis constant is defined as the substrate concentration at which half of the enzyme molecules are forming enzyme-substrate (ES) complex or concentration of the substrate when the velocity of the enzyme reaction is half the maximal possible. A smaller Km value indicates a greater affinity of the enzyme for its substrate, hence, showing a quicker reaction. The competitive inhibitor decreases the affinity of the enzyme or substrate, thus increasing the Km value.

questions.text: Select the option that is not correct with respect to enzyme action:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The substrate binds with the enzyme at its active site
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. The substrate usually binds to the enzyme's active site, where the enzymatic reaction takes place.

options.text: The addition of a lot of succinates does not reverse the inhibition of succinic dehydrogenase by malonate
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Malonate acts as a competitive inhibitor, meaning that increasing the concentration of the substrate (succinate) can indeed reverse the inhibition.

options.text: A non-competitive inhibitor binds the enzyme at a site distinct from that which binds the substrate
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Non-competitive inhibitors attach to an allosteric site, which is separate from the active site, and they inhibit enzyme activity regardless of the substrate concentration.

options.text: Malonate is a competitive inhibitor of succinic dehydrogenase
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Malonate competes with succinate for the active site on succinic dehydrogenase, acting as a competitive inhibitor.

questions.explanation: The reduction of the activity of succinate dehydrogenase by malonate is an example of competitive inhibition. Competitive inhibition is a reversible inhibition where the inhibitor competes with the normal substrate for the active site of the enzyme. A competitive inhibitor is usually similar to the normal substrate and, therefore, fits into the active site of an enzyme and binds with it. The enzyme can no longer act upon the substrate, and reaction products are not formed. Hence, the action of an enzyme may be reduced or inhibited. Since a competitive inhibitor occupies the site only temporarily, the enzyme action is not permanently affected. Thus, the addition of a lot of succinates can reverse the inhibition of succinic dehydrogenase by malonate.

questions.text: Andersen’s disease (glycogen storage disease type IV) is a condition characterized by a deficiency in glycogen branching enzymes. The absence of this enzyme would be likely to cause all of the following effects EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Decreased glycogen solubility in human cells
options.explanation: Branches make glycogen more soluble and easier to break down. Without the enzyme, glycogen chains will be long and unbranched, which makes them less soluble and harder to break down. This will lead to decreased glycogen solubility in human cells. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: The slower action of glycogen phosphorylase
options.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase is the enzyme that breaks down glycogen to glucose. Without the branching enzyme, glycogen chains will be less accessible to glycogen phosphorylase, which will slow down the action of glycogen phosphorylase. Hence, this option is incorrect.

options.text: More storage of glucose in the body
options.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase is the enzyme that breaks down glycogen to glucose. Without the branching enzyme, glycogen chains will be less accessible to glycogen phosphorylase, which will slow down the breakdown of glycogen. This will lead to less storage of glucose in the body. Hence, this option is correct

α-1,4 linkages are the main type of linkage between glucose molecules in glycogen. Without these linkages, glycogen would not be able to form. Therefore, the only effect that is not likely to be caused by the absence of glycogen branching enzyme is glycogen devoid of α-1,4 linkages.

questions.explanation: Glycogen consists entirely of glucose molecules that are linked by two types of bonds - the alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond and the alpha-1,6-glycosidic bond. The alpha-1,4-glycosidic bond is the more common bond and it gives glycogen a helical structure that is suitable for energy storage. Without these linkages, glycogen would not be able to form. Therefore, the only effect that is not likely to be caused by the absence of glycogen branching enzyme is glycogen devoid of α-1,4 linkages.

questions.text: An investigator is measuring the activity of various enzymes involved in reactions of intermediary metabolism. One of the enzymes has greatly decreased activity compared to reference values. The buffer of the assay contains citrate. Which of the following enzymes will most likely be directly affected by the use of citrate?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphatase is a positive allosteric regulator of PFK-1, so citrate would increase its activity Hence, this option is incorrect.

Isocitrate dehydrogenase is an enzyme in the citric acid cycle, and citrate is a substrate for this enzyme. Therefore, citrate would not have a significant effect on the activity of isocitrate dehydrogenase. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Citrate is a negative allosteric regulator of PFK-1, which means that it binds to the enzyme and decreases its activity. This is why the activity of PFK-1 would be greatly decreased in the presence of citrate.

Pyruvate carboxylase is an enzyme that converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate, which is a precursor to citrate. Therefore, citrate would increase the activity of pyruvate carboxylase. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: A powerful digesting enzyme that is capable of destroying a cell's internal structure is called _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lipase is an enzyme that breaks down fats into fatty acids and glycerol, but it does not specifically target cell structures like membranes or DNA.
DNase is an enzyme that digests DNA, but it does not generally destroy the entire cell's internal structure.
Phospholipase is an enzyme that hydrolyzes phospholipids, which are key components of cell membranes, thus affecting the cell’s internal structure significantly.
While both DNase and Phospholipase have roles in breaking down specific cell components, only Phospholipase directly targets the cell's membrane, impacting its overall structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Phospholipase. Phospholipase enzymes break down phospholipids, which are critical components of cell membranes. By disrupting the membrane, phospholipases can compromise the integrity of the cell's structure, potentially leading to cell lysis. Lipase, on the other hand, targets fats for digestion outside of the cellular context. DNase digests DNA, which can affect genetic material but not the structural integrity of the cell as a whole. Option D, B and C, is incorrect because DNase does not disrupt the cell membrane.

questions.text: A new drug was developed to target a specific enzyme involved in cholesterol synthesis. In a clinical trial, patients who received the drug showed a significant reduction in total cholesterol levels. Which of the following statements best explains the mechanism of action of the drug?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The drug acts as a competitive inhibitor of the enzyme
options.explanation: Correct. The drug acts as a competitive inhibitor by competing with the natural substrate for binding to the enzyme's active site, thereby decreasing the enzyme's activity and resulting in reduced cholesterol synthesis.

options.text: The drug increases the synthesis of the enzyme
options.explanation: Incorrect. Drugs typically do not increase the synthesis of enzymes directly. Instead, they may inhibit or activate enzymes, but increasing enzyme synthesis is not a common mechanism for reducing cholesterol synthesis.

options.text: The drug enhances the binding affinity of the enzyme to its substrate
options.explanation: Incorrect. Enhancing the binding affinity would likely increase the enzyme's activity, which is contrary to the observed reduction in cholesterol levels.

options.text: The drug allosterically modulates the activity of the enzyme
options.explanation: Incorrect. Allosteric modulation involves binding to a site other than the active site, which can either inhibit or activate an enzyme. However, this option suggests permanent inhibition, which is not consistent with the observed temporary reduction in cholesterol levels.

questions.explanation: The drug acts as a competitive inhibitor of the enzyme. Competitive inhibitors compete with the substrate for the active site of the enzyme, thereby reducing the enzyme's activity. In this case, the drug is specifically designed to target the enzyme involved in cholesterol synthesis, suggesting that it competes with the substrate for the enzyme's active site.

questions.text: An enzyme catalyzes a reaction at a rate of 1000 molecules per second at 25°C. If the temperature is increased to 45°C, which of the following is the most likely outcome?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The enzyme activity will remain the same
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Enzyme activity does not remain constant with temperature changes; it generally increases up to an optimum point and decreases past that due to denaturation.
This option is incorrect. While enzyme activity decreases beyond the optimum temperature, it initially increases as the temperature rises towards the optimum range (usually around 37-40°C).

options.text: The enzyme activity will increase initially and then decrease
options.explanation: This option is correct. As the temperature rises from 25°C to 45°C, enzyme activity initially increases towards the optimum temperature range (37-40°C) and then decreases due to enzyme denaturation beyond this range.

This option is incorrect. Although enzyme activity increases up to the optimum temperature, it decreases past this point due to denaturation, thus it doesn’t continuously increase with rising temperatures.

questions.explanation: The enzyme activity will increase initially and then decrease is correct. As the temperature is increased from 25°C to 45°C, the enzyme's activity will first increase upto 40°C, and then will decrease. Because enzyme's activity increases with increase in temperature upto 37-40°C, which is optimum temperature. Beyond this temperature, enzyme's activity decreases because denaturation of enzyme occurs.

questions.text: An experiment was conducted to determine the effect of enzyme concentration on the rate of a reaction. The reaction was carried out with varying enzyme concentrations, while keeping the substrate concentration constant. The results showed that the reaction rate increased linearly with increasing enzyme concentration until a certain point, after which further increases in enzyme concentration did not significantly affect the reaction rate. What phenomenon does this observation most likely represent?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct. Saturation kinetics refers to a situation where the reaction rate reaches a maximum, regardless of further increases in the concentration of an enzyme. When the enzyme concentration is low, the reaction rate increases linearly with enzyme concentration. However, once all the substrate molecules are occupied by enzymes, the reaction rate plateaus, indicating saturation.
Incorrect. Cooperative binding results in a sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve, not a linear increase followed by a plateau as seen in the experiment.
Incorrect. Irreversible inhibition would lead to a decline in reaction rate, not a plateau as observed in the experiment.
Incorrect. Enzyme denaturation would result in a drop in reaction rate, not a leveling off as seen in the experiment.

questions.explanation: Saturation kinetics. Saturation kinetics refers to a situation where the reaction rate reaches a maximum, regardless of further increases in the concentration of an enzyme. When the enzyme concentration is low, the reaction rate increases linearly with enzyme concentration. However, once all the substrate molecules are occupied by enzymes, the reaction rate plateaus, indicating saturation.

questions.text: Which of these statements about enzymes is true?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Enzymes accelerate reactions by lowering the activation energy
options.explanation: Enzymes accelerate reactions by lowering the activation energy: Enzymes act as catalysts in biological systems, and one of their primary roles is to accelerate chemical reactions. They achieve this by lowering the activation energy required for the reaction to occur. Enzymes provide an alternative pathway for the reaction, allowing it to proceed more rapidly.

options.text: Enzymes are proteins whose three-dimensional form is key to their function
options.explanation: Enzymes are proteins whose three-dimensional form is key to their function: Enzymes are typically proteins, and their three-dimensional structure is crucial for their proper functioning. The active site of an enzyme, where the substrate binds, is typically formed by specific amino acid residues in a precise arrangement. This three-dimensional structure allows enzymes to interact with their substrates and carry out their catalytic function.

options.text: Enzymes do not alter the overall change in free energy for a reaction
options.explanation: Enzymes do not alter the overall change in free energy for a reaction: Enzymes do not alter the overall change in free energy (ΔG) for a reaction. They facilitate the conversion of substrates to products by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy, but they do not change the overall energy difference between reactants and products. Enzymes only speed up reactions that are thermodynamically favorable; they do not alter the energetics of the reaction itself.

Therefore, all of these statements are true about enzymes, making option D the correct answer. Enzymes lower the activation energy, their three-dimensional structure is crucial, and they do not alter the overall change in free energy for a reaction.

questions.explanation: The correct option is D) All of these.A) Enzymes accelerate reactions by lowering the activation energy: Enzymes are biological catalysts that provide an alternative, lower-energy pathway for the reaction to proceed, thereby speeding up its rate.B) Enzymes are proteins whose three-dimensional form is key to their function: The unique 3D structure, determined by the amino acid sequence, creates the specific active site where the substrate binds, enabling catalysis.C) Enzymes do not alter the overall change in free energy for a reaction: Enzymes only change the reaction rate, not the difference in free energy between the reactants and products, which means they do not affect the reaction's equilibrium or spontaneity.

questions.text: When 3. Increase in concentration and the rate of the reaction decreases, it is called;
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Competitive inhibition occurs when a molecule, called the inhibitor, competes with the substrate for binding to the active site of the enzyme. The inhibitor molecule is often structurally similar to the substrate and can bind to the active site of the enzyme, preventing the substrate from binding. This competition for the active site reduces the effective concentration of the substrate available for reaction, leading to a decrease in the rate of the reaction. In this case, the increase in product concentration does not directly affect the rate of the reaction.

Feedback inhibition, also known as end-product inhibition, is a regulatory mechanism in which the final product of a metabolic pathway acts as an inhibitor for an enzyme earlier in the pathway. When the concentration of the product accumulates, it binds to the enzyme responsible for its synthesis, inhibiting its activity. This inhibition slows down the reaction and prevents the overproduction of the product. In this case, the increase in product concentration directly affects the rate of the reaction, causing it to decrease.

Non-competitive inhibition occurs when an inhibitor molecule binds to a site on the enzyme distinct from the active site, known as the allosteric site. This binding induces a conformational change in the enzyme that reduces its catalytic activity. Non-competitive inhibition can occur regardless of the concentration of the product. It does not directly relate to an increase in product concentration causing a decrease in the rate of the reaction.

In the given scenario, where an increase in product concentration leads to a decrease in the rate of the reaction, both competitive inhibition (option A) and feedback inhibition (option B) can apply. Therefore, the correct answer is D. A and B.

questions.explanation: In the given scenario, where an increase in product concentration leads to a decrease in the rate of the reaction, both competitive inhibition (option A) and feedback inhibition (option B) can apply. Therefore, the correct answer is D. A and B.

questions.text: 1 and 2 together with the enzyme molecule form an enzyme-substrate complex. What is incorrect regarding the ES complex?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The formation of the ES complex is indeed a reversible process. When the substrate encounters the enzyme's active site, they bind together to form the ES complex. This complex can then undergo a reverse reaction, resulting in the dissociation of the substrate from the enzyme. The reversibility of the ES complex allows the enzyme to catalyze the reaction repeatedly by binding and releasing substrates.

options.text: Substrate reacts with the enzyme's charge bearing site
options.explanation: Option B is also correct. The substrate interacts with the enzyme's active site, which is a specific region on the enzyme's surface that accommodates the substrate. The active site typically contains specific amino acid residues that form complementary interactions with the substrate, facilitating the catalytic reaction.

Option C is correct as well. The ES complex, like most biomolecular complexes, has a three-dimensional structure. The interaction between the substrate and the enzyme occurs through various non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions. These interactions contribute to the overall three-dimensional shape and stability of the ES complex.

options.text: ES complex always proceeds to form the final product
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect. The ES complex does not always proceed to form the final product. The fate of the ES complex depends on various factors, including the concentration of substrates, presence of inhibitors, and reaction conditions. While the ES complex can lead to the formation of the final product in many cases, it can also revert back to the substrate and enzyme or form an enzyme-inhibitor complex, preventing the reaction from proceeding.

questions.text: Enzymes present in saliva will be denatured when
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enzymes present in saliva can be denatured when one eats very hot food. Denaturation refers to the alteration of the enzyme's structure, which can result in the loss of its catalytic activity. High temperatures can disrupt the weak bonds that maintain the specific three-dimensional structure of enzymes, leading to denaturation. When enzymes in saliva are exposed to very hot food, the heat can cause their structure to unfold or change, rendering them inactive or less effective in catalyzing reactions.

Option B is not directly related to the denaturation of enzymes in saliva. Spitting does not typically cause denaturation. However, it is worth mentioning that enzymes in saliva can be inactivated or degraded over time when exposed to environmental factors such as temperature, pH, and microbial action. So, if saliva is exposed to unfavorable conditions for an extended period, the enzymes present in it may undergo degradation or become ineffective.

options.text: Eating the food that contains bacteria
options.explanation: Option C is not directly related to the denaturation of enzymes in saliva. The presence of bacteria in food does not necessarily lead to the denaturation of enzymes in saliva. However, enzymes in saliva can play a role in the initial breakdown of food components, and bacterial contamination of food can impact the overall digestive process. Bacteria may produce their own enzymes that can interact with the enzymes in saliva, potentially affecting their activity or effectiveness. This interaction does not involve denaturation but rather the influence of bacterial enzymes on the overall digestive process.

options.text: There is no food present for enzymes to work on
options.explanation: Option D is not directly related to the denaturation of enzymes in saliva. When there is no food present for enzymes to work on, they may not be actively engaged in catalyzing reactions, but their denaturation does not occur due to the absence of food. Denaturation typically happens when enzymes are exposed to adverse conditions such as extreme temperatures, pH extremes, or chemical denaturants.

questions.text: On eating citrus fruits like lemon, salivary amylase is denatured temporarily due to alter in its pH. What makes its activity to recover?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The buffering capacity of saliva helps in the recovery of salivary amylase activity after it is temporarily denatured due to a change in pH caused by consuming citrus fruits like lemon. Saliva contains bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻) that act as a buffer, helping to maintain the pH within a certain range. When the pH in the mouth becomes acidic after consuming citrus fruits, the buffering capacity of saliva helps to restore the pH to a more optimal level for salivary amylase activity. The buffer system in saliva helps to minimize the pH changes and provides an environment suitable for the enzyme to regain its activity.

options.text: Drinking water to neutralize the pH
options.explanation: Drinking water can help dilute the acidic components and provide temporary relief from the acidic sensation caused by consuming citrus fruits.

options.text: Eating watermelon or similar food which are basic in nature
options.explanation: Eating watermelon or similar foods that have a basic or alkaline nature can help in neutralizing the acidic pH in the mouth to some extent. These foods contain natural compounds, such as citrulline in watermelon, that can have a mild alkalizing effect. By consuming such foods, the pH in the mouth can be partially restored, creating a more favorable environment for salivary amylase activity.
All of the mentioned factors contribute to the recovery of salivary amylase activity after it is temporarily denatured due to the change in pH caused by consuming citrus fruits.

questions.explanation: Salivary amylase is active at a near-neutral pH (around 6.7) and is temporarily denatured by the low pH of citrus fruits. All three factors help the enzyme recover its activity:The buffering capacity of saliva (A) neutralizes the acid over time, returning the pH to the optimal range.Drinking water (B) dilutes the acid and helps wash it away, speeding up the neutralization process.Eating basic food (C) introduces substances that chemically counteract the acid, rapidly restoring the optimal pH for the enzyme to refold and become active again.

questions.text: An enzymatic activity is decreased when substrate is added for the reaction to occur. However, when more of the substrate is added the activity is back to normal. How can this behaviour be explained?

options.text: The enzyme was inhabited by the non-competitive inhibitor

options.text: Adding yet more substrate saturated the enzyme

options.text: Different pH but same temperature optima
options.explanation: Many enzymes share similar temperature optima but have different pH optima, reflecting the diverse environments in which they operate.
While pH optima can differ widely among enzymes, temperature optima are often similar, especially within the same organism.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that enzymes generally have different pH but the same temperature optima. Enzymes are proteins that catalyze biochemical reactions, and they are highly specific to the conditions in which they operate. While the optimal pH can vary widely between enzymes, reflecting their adaptation to specific environments (e.g., acidic conditions in the stomach for pepsin), the optimal temperature is often similar for enzymes within the same organism, as they are adapted to the organism's body temperature. Options A and B are incorrect because enzymes do not typically share the same pH optima. Option D is incorrect because, although pH optima can differ, temperature optima are usually consistent within an organism.

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding enzyme inhibition is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Non-competitive inhibition of an enzyme can be overcome by adding a large amount of substrate
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Non-competitive inhibition occurs when an inhibitor binds to an enzyme at a site other than the active site. This changes the enzyme's shape and reduces its activity without being affected by substrate concentration.

options.text: Competitive inhibition is seen when a substrate competes with an inhibitor to bind to an enzyme
options.explanation: This statement is correct. In competitive inhibition, an inhibitor and the substrate compete for the same active site on the enzyme. Increasing the substrate concentration can overcome this inhibition.

options.text: Competitive inhibition is seen when the substrate and the inhibitor do not compete
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. This description contradicts the definition of competitive inhibition, where the substrate and inhibitor compete for the active site.

options.text: Non-competitive inhibitors often bind to the enzyme irreversibly
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Non-competitive inhibitors typically bind reversibly to an enzyme at a site other than the active site, affecting enzyme activity without permanently altering the enzyme.

questions.explanation: This statement is correct. In competitive inhibition, an inhibitor competes with the substrate for binding to the active site of the enzyme. This competition affects the enzyme's ability to interact with the substrate and carry out its catalytic function. Therefore, the correct answer is B, as it accurately describes competitive inhibition, where the substrate and inhibitor compete for binding to the active site of the enzyme.

questions.text: All enzymes of the TCA cycle are located in the mitochondrial matrix except one which is located in inner mitochondrial membranes in eukaryotes and the cytosol in prokaryotes. This enzyme is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Lactate dehydrogenase is not part of the TCA cycle. It is involved in converting pyruvate to lactate during anaerobic conditions, and does not localize within the mitochondria.
Incorrect. Although isocitrate dehydrogenase is part of the TCA cycle, it is located in the mitochondrial matrix, not the inner mitochondrial membrane or cytosol.
Incorrect. Malate dehydrogenase is an enzyme of the TCA cycle found in the mitochondrial matrix, not in the inner mitochondrial membrane or the cytosol.

Correct. Succinate dehydrogenase is unique among TCA cycle enzymes as it is located in the inner mitochondrial membrane in eukaryotes and the cytosol in prokaryotes. It also plays a key role in the electron transport chain.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. Succinate dehydrogenase is the only enzyme of the TCA cycle that is embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane in eukaryotes. In prokaryotes, it is found in the cytosol. It plays a crucial role in both the TCA cycle and the electron transport chain. Therefore, the correct answer is D, as succinate dehydrogenase is the enzyme in the TCA cycle that deviates from the mitochondrial matrix location and is instead found in the inner mitochondrial membrane in eukaryotes and the cytosol in prokaryotes.

questions.text: The transition state structure of the substrate formed during an enzymatic reaction is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect: This option is contradictory. A transient state cannot be stable. The transition state is indeed transient, existing only briefly, but it is not stable.
Incorrect: This is contradictory. A permanent state implies stability. The transition state is not permanent; it is a fleeting, unstable configuration that occurs during the reaction.
Correct: The transition state structure is transient, meaning it exists only briefly, and unstable, representing a high-energy state as the reaction progresses.
Incorrect: The transition state is neither permanent nor stable. It is a short-lived, high-energy configuration in an enzymatic reaction.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. The transition state structure during an enzymatic reaction is transient, meaning it exists only briefly, and it is also unstable, representing a high-energy state as the reaction proceeds. Therefore, the correct answer is C. The transition state structure of the substrate formed during an enzymatic reaction is transient and unstable.

questions.text: Enzyme-driven metabolic pathways can be made more efficient by
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Grouping enzymes into multienzyme free-floating complexes
options.explanation: This strategy involves assembling multiple enzymes into complexes, allowing them to work closely together. This proximity facilitates efficient substrate transfer between enzymes, enhancing the overall metabolic pathway.

options.text: Concentrating enzymes within specific cellular compartments
options.explanation: By localising enzymes to specific cellular regions, the concentration of substrates and enzymes increases, promoting more frequent and efficient interactions. This spatial organisation helps streamline metabolic processes.

options.text: Fixing enzymes into membranes so they are adjacent to each other
options.explanation: Membrane fixation ensures that enzymes are nearby, promoting rapid transfer of intermediates between them. This arrangement facilitates coordinated catalysis and can enhance the efficiency of metabolic reactions.

The correct answer indicates that combining these strategies can collectively contribute to making enzyme-driven metabolic pathways more efficient. Grouping enzymes into complexes, concentrating them in cellular compartments, and fixing them in membranes are complementary approaches that work together to optimise metabolic processes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer indicates that combining these strategies can collectively contribute to making enzyme-driven metabolic pathways more efficient. Grouping enzymes into complexes, concentrating them in cellular compartments, and fixing them in membranes are complementary approaches that work together to optimise metabolic processes.

questions.text: Consider the following statements.l. A coenzyme of metal ions that are tightly bound to an enzyme protein is called the prosthetic group.ll. A complete catalytic active enzyme with its bound prosthetic group is called apoenzyme.Select the correct option.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both A and B are false
options.explanation: As both statements I and II are false, this is the correct option. The prosthetic group is a non-protein component tightly bound to the enzyme, and the complete, catalytically active enzyme with its bound prosthetic group is called a holoenzyme, not an apoenzyme.

options.text: A is false but B is true
options.explanation: Statement II is also false, as the complete, catalytically active enzyme with its bound prosthetic group is called a holoenzyme, not an apoenzyme.

options.text: Both A and B are true
options.explanation: The prosthetic group is a non-protein component tightly bound to the enzyme (not only metal ions), and the complete, catalytically active enzyme with its bound prosthetic group is called a holoenzyme, not an apoenzyme.

options.text: A is true but B is false
options.explanation: Statement I is false, as the prosthetic group is a non-protein component tightly bound to the enzyme (not only metal ions).

questions.explanation: l. A coenzyme of metal ions that is tightly bound to enzyme protein is called the prosthetic group: This statement is false. The prosthetic group is a non-protein component that is tightly and permanently attached to the enzyme. While a prosthetic group can be a coenzyme, it is not exclusive to metal ions.ll. A complete catalytically active enzyme with its bound prosthetic group is called an apoenzyme: This statement is false. An apoenzyme is the protein component of an enzyme without its bound cofactor or prosthetic group. A complete, catalytically active enzyme with its bound cofactor or prosthetic group is called a holoenzyme. So, Option A is correct, as both statements are false.

Lipases are enzymes that hydrolyze fats into glycerol and fatty acids. They are not involved in digesting other enzymes.

This is the correct option because they are enzymes that digest other enzymes by breaking down peptides into amino acids.
Aldolases are enzymes involved in the breakdown of sugars, particularly in glycolysis. They do not digest other enzymes.

The hardness of dry seeds is related to their state of dormancy but doesn't directly explain their ability to endure higher temperatures.

questions.text: A student conducts an experiment to test the efficiency of a certain enzyme. Which would probably not result in a change in the enzyme's efficiency?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Adding an acidic solution to the setup
options.explanation: Enzymes have specific pH optima; an acidic solution can alter the enzyme's shape and reduce its efficiency.

options.text: Adding more substrate but not enzyme
options.explanation: Increasing substrate concentration can increase efficiency (reaction rate) until it reaches the saturation point; after that, the efficiency doesn't change
Temperature can increase reaction rates until a point, but high temperatures may denature the enzyme, affecting efficiency.
Keeping the environmental factors constant ensures that the enzyme operates within its optimal range, maintaining efficiency.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D, maintaining constant environmental conditions. Enzymes are sensitive to changes in their environment. Adding an acidic solution (Option A) can affect the enzyme's structure and function due to pH changes. Adding more substrate (Option B) can increase the reaction rate until saturation is reached, altering efficiency. Increasing temperature (Option C) can enhance enzyme activity to a point, but excessive heat can denature the enzyme, reducing efficiency. Therefore, all these factors would likely change the enzyme's efficiency.

questions.text: Enzyme activity can be blocked or retarded on minute disturbance in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Density does not directly influence enzyme activity unless it affects temperature or pH, which are critical for enzyme function.
Enzymes have a specific pH range in which they function best. Even small deviations from this optimum pH can significantly reduce enzyme activity or cause denaturation.
Temperature does affect enzyme activity, but enzymes are generally more sensitive to minute changes in pH than temperature.
While substrate concentration affects enzyme activity, enzymes are not as sensitive to small changes in concentration as they are to changes in pH.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is optimum pH. Enzymes are highly sensitive to changes in pH, and even small deviations can lead to a significant decrease in their activity or complete denaturation. This is because pH affects the ionization of amino acids at the active site, which is crucial for enzyme-substrate binding. While temperature and substrate concentration also affect enzyme activity, a minute change in pH generally has a more immediate and pronounced effect. Density is not directly related to enzyme activity unless it influences other factors such as pH or temperature.

questions.text: Which one of the graphs below shows the effect of pH on the reaction velocity of a typical enzyme?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This graph represents a constant or linear relationship, which is not typical for enzyme activity as it changes with pH.
This graph displays a bell-shaped curve, indicating that enzyme activity peaks at an optimal pH. This is the typical response of enzymes to changes in pH.
This graph suggests that enzyme activity continually increases with pH, which is atypical as enzymes generally have a specific optimal pH range.
This graph indicates a continuous decrease in activity with increasing pH, which does not reflect the typical peak and decline pattern seen in enzyme activity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B. Enzymes typically have an optimal pH at which their activity is highest, and this is often depicted as a bell-shaped curve. This curve shows that enzyme activity increases as pH approaches the optimal value, then decreases as pH moves away from it. Option A suggests a constant or linear relationship, which is not typical. Option C implies that enzyme activity continues to increase with pH indefinitely, which is not characteristic of enzymes, as they generally have an optimal pH. Option D suggests activity decreases with increasing pH, lacking the characteristic peak of enzyme activity.

questions.text: Which molecule is a cofactor used by dehydrogenase enzymes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Coenzyme A is primarily involved in the transfer of acyl groups and is not typically involved in the redox reactions catalyzed by dehydrogenase enzymes.
Cytochromes function in the electron transport chain and are not directly used by dehydrogenase enzymes as cofactors in oxidation-reduction reactions.
Lactate is a metabolite, often the product of enzymatic reactions, but it does not function as a cofactor for dehydrogenase enzymes.

NAD (Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) is a common cofactor used by dehydrogenase enzymes to facilitate electron transfer during oxidation-reduction reactions, thus playing a crucial role in these processes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is NAD. Dehydrogenase enzymes are responsible for catalyzing oxidation-reduction reactions, where they typically remove hydrogen from a substrate. NAD (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) acts as an electron carrier in these reactions, accepting electrons and becoming reduced to NADH. This makes NAD an essential cofactor for many dehydrogenase enzymes.Coenzyme A is involved in the transfer of acyl groups and does not play a role in the electron transfer reactions catalyzed by dehydrogenases. Cytochromes are part of the electron transport chain and not directly used by dehydrogenases. Lactate is a metabolic product, not a cofactor for enzyme activity.

questions.text: In the following graph, each of the curves P, Q, and R represents an enzyme-controlled reaction in one of the following situations:(i) with the enzyme uninhibited(ii) with a non-competitive inhibitor added to the enzyme(iii) with a competitive inhibitor added to the enzymeWhich curve represents each situation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Curve P shows the highest reaction rate and represents the uninhibited enzyme. Curve R, which has a lower maximum rate, indicates non-competitive inhibition. Curve Q, reaching the same maximum rate as P but requiring higher substrate concentration, indicates competitive inhibition.
Curve Q cannot represent noncompetitive inhibition, as it reaches the same maximum rate as P, and R cannot represent competitive inhibition, as it reaches a lower rate.
Curve Q does not represent the uninhibited reaction, as it requires increased substrate concentration to reach the maximum rate, typical of competitive inhibition.
This option misidentifies Curve P, which actually represents the uninhibited reaction with the highest reaction rate, not competitive inhibition.

questions.explanation: In an enzyme-controlled reaction graph, the uninhibited enzyme reaction is represented by the curve with the highest maximum reaction rate, which is Curve P. A non-competitive inhibitor decreases the maximum reaction rate regardless of substrate concentration, which is represented by Curve R. A competitive inhibitor competes with the substrate for the active site, which can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration, allowing the reaction to eventually reach the same maximum rate as the uninhibited reaction, represented by Curve Q. Thus, the correct correspondence is (i) Curve P, (ii) Curve Q, and (iii) Curve R.

questions.text: In the graph, X represents the relationship between an enzyme and substrate concentration under optimal conditions and without an inhibitor. The same experiment is repeated in the presence of a fixed, low concentration of a competitive inhibitor.Which curve represents the expected result?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This curve indicates that in the presence of a competitive inhibitor, a higher substrate concentration is necessary to achieve the same reaction rate as without the inhibitor, maintaining the same Vmax.
This curve inaccurately suggests that the presence of a competitive inhibitor does not require an increase in substrate concentration to maintain the same reaction rate.
This curve reflects a non-competitive inhibition scenario, where the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) is reduced, but substrate concentration has no effect on overcoming the inhibitor.
This curve erroneously suggests that the reaction rate decreases at higher substrate concentrations, which is not typical for competitive inhibition.

questions.explanation: In the presence of a competitive inhibitor, the enzyme can still reach its maximum reaction rate (Vmax) if enough substrate is added to outcompete the inhibitor. Thus, the curve will shift to the right, indicating a higher substrate concentration is required to achieve the same reaction rate as without the inhibitor. Option A reflects this scenario. Option B does not show the necessary increase in substrate concentration to overcome the inhibitor. Option C suggests a non-competitive inhibition scenario where the inhibitor affects Vmax, and Option D does not align with typical enzyme kinetics in the presence of a competitive inhibitor.

questions.text: Which enzyme shows the most substrate specificity?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lipase catalyzes the breakdown of various fats into fatty acids and glycerol, and though it is specific to lipid substrates, it acts on a variety of lipid types.
Protease is an enzyme that cleaves peptide bonds, aiding in protein digestion, and is specific to protein substrates but not to a single protein type.
Sucrase demonstrates high substrate specificity by exclusively catalyzing the hydrolysis of sucrose into glucose and fructose.
Amylase acts on starches, breaking them down into sugars, but can target a variety of polysaccharides, indicating broader substrate specificity.

questions.explanation: Sucrase is highly specific as it acts on only one substrate, sucrose, converting it to glucose and fructose. This high specificity makes sucrase the most substrate-specific among the given options.Lipase, protease, and amylase also show substrate specificity, but they act on broader categories of substrates. Lipase works on various fats, protease on proteins, and amylase on a range of polysaccharides, making them less specific compared to sucrase.

questions.text: The 4 acids shown below form part of an enzyme-catalyzed sequence.Oxoglutaric acid (alpha-ketoglutaric acid) ---> (enzyme1) ---> succinic acid ---> (enzyme 2) ---> 2H ---> Fumaric acid ---> (enzyme 3) ---> malic acidThe addition of malonic acid results in no change in the concentration of oxoglutaric acid, an accumulation of succinic acid, and a near absence of both fumaric acid and malic acid. Further addition of fumaric acid results in the formation of malic acid.What does this information indicate about malonic acid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is an inhibitor of enzyme 1
options.explanation: If malonic acid inhibited enzyme 1, we would see an accumulation of oxoglutaric acid. However, the concentration of oxoglutaric acid remains unchanged, indicating that enzyme 1 is not inhibited.

options.text: It catalyses the formation of succinic acid
options.explanation: Malonic acid is not a catalyst. It is acting as an inhibitor in this sequence, specifically affecting the conversion of succinic acid to fumaric acid.

options.text: It is an inhibitor of enzyme 2
options.explanation: Malonic acid inhibits enzyme 2, which is responsible for converting succinic acid to fumaric acid. This is supported by the accumulation of succinic acid and the lack of fumaric acid.
Malonic acid does not directly react with fumaric acid. Instead, it inhibits enzyme 2, which affects the formation of fumaric acid.

questions.explanation: Malonic acid acts as an inhibitor of enzyme 2, which is responsible for converting succinic acid to fumaric acid. The accumulation of succinic acid and the near absence of fumaric acid and malic acid indicate that the pathway is blocked at this step. The addition of fumaric acid results in the formation of malic acid, confirming that the inhibition is specific to enzyme 2 and not downstream in the pathway. Thus, Option C is correct. The other options are incorrect as they do not align with the observed effects of malonic acid on the metabolic sequence.

questions.text: Curve X represents the course of an enzyme-catalyzed reaction under optimum conditions.Curve Y shows the action of the same enzyme on the same substrate but with one alteration to the reaction conditions.Which of the following factors, operating to a constant extent throughout the experiment, could give the results shown by curve Y?

options.explanation: While a higher pH can affect enzyme activity, it usually changes enzyme conformation, which might not align with the constant pattern shown by curve Y.
Lowering temperature decreases kinetic energy and slows down reactions, but the pattern in curve Y suggests a consistent alteration, more in line with inhibition rather than temperature effect.
An end-product inhibitor leads to feedback inhibition, reducing enzyme activity and resulting in a slower reaction rate as depicted by curve Y.
Increasing substrate concentration typically speeds up the reaction until enzyme saturation, contrary to the slower rate suggested by curve Y.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option C: An end-product inhibitor. End-product inhibition is a form of feedback inhibition where the end product of a reaction inhibits the enzyme that produces it, thereby slowing down the reaction rate as depicted by curve Y. This aligns with the results shown by curve Y, where the reaction proceeds at a slower rate than under optimum conditions.Option A is incorrect because while pH changes can affect enzyme activity, a higher pH would not typically lead to the observed pattern unless it significantly affected enzyme stability.Option B is incorrect as a lower temperature would slow down the reaction due to decreased kinetic energy, but this scenario is not consistent with the type of inhibition shown in the curves.Option D is incorrect because an increased substrate concentration would typically increase the reaction rate, not decrease it.

questions.text: Which graph represents the change in substrate concentration during the course of an enzyme controlled reaction?

questions.text: The diagram illustrates energy changes in an enzyme-controlled reaction.Which of the following represents the lowering of activation energy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: X likely indicates overall energy change (between reactants and products).

Z typically represents the activation energy without enzyme involvement. It shows the energy required in the uncatalyzed reaction but not the change itself.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of feedback inhibition in a metabolic pathway?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A non-competitive inhibitor binds irreversibly to the substrate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Non-competitive inhibitors bind to an enzyme at a location other than the active site and do not bind to the substrate itself. This mechanism is unrelated to feedback inhibition.

options.text: An enzyme-controlled reaction slows down as end product accumulates
options.explanation: Correct. In feedback inhibition, the accumulation of an end product inhibits an enzyme involved in the pathway, reducing or halting the reaction's rate.

options.text: There is competition between two enzymes for a common substrate
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. It describes competitive inhibition, where enzymes compete for the same substrate, which is different from feedback inhibition.

options.text: Variations in enzyme concentration affect the rate of the reaction it catalyses
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it relates to changes in enzyme concentration impacting reaction rates, not feedback inhibition which involves regulation by end products.

questions.explanation: Feedback inhibition is a regulatory mechanism where the accumulation of the end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an enzyme involved in that pathway, thus controlling the pathway's activity. Option B correctly describes this process, where the reaction slows as the end product accumulates. Option A is incorrect because non-competitive inhibition involves the inhibitor binding elsewhere on the enzyme, not directly related to feedback inhibition. Option C describes competitive inhibition, not feedback inhibition. Option D relates to enzyme kinetics and concentration, which doesn't pertain to feedback inhibition.

questions.text: Each graph represents the rate of a reaction of an uninhibited enzyme and that of the same enzyme in presence of a constant amount of either a competitive or a noncompetitive inhibitor.Which graph is correctly labelled?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Graph A shows an increase in reaction rate with substrate concentration, but it does not clearly depict the effects of either competitive or noncompetitive inhibition.
Graph B suggests a competitive inhibition scenario, where increasing substrate concentration allows the reaction rate to reach the maximum, but it does not match the classic pattern correctly.
Graph C shows a reaction rate that reaches the maximum, which shouldnt be the case in non-competitive inhibition, in which reaction rate plateaus below the maximum.
Graph D correctly represents competitive inhibition, where the reaction rate can approach the maximum with high substrate concentration.

questions.explanation: In competitive inhibition, the inhibitor competes with the substrate for the active site of the enzyme. This means that the reaction rate can eventually reach the same maximum rate as the uninhibited enzyme, given sufficient substrate concentration. Graph D correctly shows this pattern.In contrast, noncompetitive inhibitors bind to an enzyme at a different site, reducing the overall number of active enzymes regardless of substrate concentration. This leads to a reduced maximum rate, as depicted incorrectly by Graph C, which misrepresents noncompetitive inhibition. Graphs A and B do not accurately match the classic depictions of enzyme inhibition patterns.

questions.text: The reaction rate of salivary amylase with starch decreases as the concentration of chloride ions is reduced. Which of the following describes the role of the chloride ions?
